ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for winter melon tea specifically; mapped to broader beverage food product class. FoodOn does not currently enumerate traditional Chinese herbal or sugar-based beverages at the specific level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: Winter melon tea is a sugar-sweetened beverage made by dissolving caramelized winter melon and sugar syrup in water. Mapped to FoodEx2 sugar-sweetened beverages category A0FJP as the closest defensible code; no specific FoodEx2 entry exists for this traditional preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for winter melon tea as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of winter melon tea is approximately 250 ml (one glass), providing around 112 kcal and 28 g of carbohydrates, predominantly from added brown and rock sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, fruit-flavored drink, powder, with added sodium, prepared with water — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with winter melon (wax gourd, raw) USDA FoodData Central entry 168412
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for traditional winter melon tea (冬瓜茶). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for wax gourd / winter melon (raw, FDC ID 168412) combined with USDA data for sugar-sweetened beverages and brown sugar (FDC ID 169655) to reflect the diluted, sugar-dominant composition of the prepared drink. The input record values (45 kcal, 0.1 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 11.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g) are consistent with this composition and are retained. Singapore HPB database does not list this specific beverage.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for sugar-sweetened beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for traditional winter melon tea (冬瓜茶) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated by analogy to sugar-sweetened beverages with similar sucrose/brown sugar composition (GI range 58–70 per University of Sydney GI Database). Glycemic load per 250 ml serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 28.0) / 100 = 18.2. This is consistent with the medium-GI, medium-GL classification for a sugar-sweetened drink.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (5 mg per 100 g); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Winter melon tea is very low in sodium and does not contribute meaningfully to sodium load; however, individuals with hypertension should be mindful of total caloric and sugar intake, as excess sugar consumption can indirectly elevate blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar content (10.5 g sugar per 100 g; ~26 g per 250 ml serving) with medium glycemic index of 65 and glycemic load of 18.2 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Winter melon tea should be avoided or strictly limited by individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its high added sugar content and medium-to-high glycemic load per serving, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; a reduced-sugar or sugar-free version is strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar intake linked to elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular risk; negligible saturated fat and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While winter melon tea is virtually fat-free and very low in sodium, its high added sugar content (predominantly brown sugar and rock sugar) contributes to excess caloric intake and may raise triglyceride levels, which is a recognized cardiovascular risk factor; consumption should be limited to occasional servings.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar intake may promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Winter melon itself contains trace antioxidants and minerals, but the high added sugar content of this beverage may be pro-inflammatory in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and should be consumed in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Winter Melon Tea (冬瓜茶, Dōngguā chá, Dong Gua Cha) is a traditional sweet beverage originating from China and widely popular across Taiwan, Singapore, and Southeast Asia. It is prepared by simmering cubed fresh winter melon (Benincasa hispida, also known as wax gourd) together with brown sugar and rock sugar over low heat until the mixture caramelizes and reduces into a thick, dark amber syrup or concentrated sugar blocks. To serve, the syrup or blocks are dissolved in cold or warm water, producing a deep amber-coloured drink. Despite its name, winter melon tea contains no tea leaves whatsoever. The flavour profile is intensely sweet with rich malty, caramelized, and slightly roasted-nut notes derived from the Maillard reaction during prolonged sugar cooking. The texture is smooth and liquid. Nutritionally, per 100 ml the drink provides approximately 45 kcal, 11.2 g carbohydrates (of which 10.5 g are sugars), 0.1 g protein, and negligible fat. It is very low in sodium (5 mg per 100 g) and contains trace amounts of vitamin C, potassium, calcium, and B vitamins contributed by the winter melon. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) with a glycemic load of approximately 18 per 250 ml serving, making it unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or those managing blood glucose. In Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), winter melon is prized for its cooling and diuretic properties, and the tea is traditionally consumed as a summer refreshment to relieve heat. It is commonly served chilled over ice and pairs well with salty or fried snacks. The beverage is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in winter melon tea?
Is winter melon tea good for diabetics?
Does winter melon tea contain caffeine?
Is winter melon tea the same as bubble tea?
What is the glycemic index of winter melon tea?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for winter melon tea exists in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for wax gourd (raw) and brown sugar, consistent with the diluted sugar-dominant composition of the prepared beverage. The input record values are plausible and retained. GI is estimated by analogy to sugar-sweetened beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero (0.0) for this beverage: winter melon tea is a water-based sugar-sweetened drink with no added fat ingredients; fat content is naturally zero by preparation. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all 0.0 for the same reason — confirmed zero by composition. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: plant-based beverage, naturally zero. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: winter melon contains negligible beta-carotene; confirmed trace/zero per USDA FoodData Central wax gourd entry. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: plant-based beverage, naturally zero. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: no animal-derived or fortified ingredients; naturally zero. water_g estimated at 88.5 g per 100 g based on composition (100 − 11.2 carbohydrate − 0.1 protein − 0.0 fat − 0.1 fibre − 0.005 sodium equivalent ≈ 88.5). No exact FoodOn URI exists for winter melon tea; mapped to broader beverage food product class. FoodEx2 code A0FJP (sugar-sweetened beverages) used as closest defensible analogue. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for sugar-sweetened beverages with similar sucrose/brown sugar composition. Glycemic load per 250 ml serving = (65 × 28.0) / 100 = 18.2. The input record listed duplicate portions (two identical entries for 250 ml); deduplicated to one canonical serving. Input source 'Journal of Food and Nutrition Research' (sciepub.com) is not on the approved source list and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the primary nutrient evidence source.
